input: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: Suzy and Billy are working on a project that is very important for our nation's security. The boss tells them both: "Be sure that you are here at exactly 9 am. It is absolutely essential that you arrive at that time." Both Billy and Suzy arrive at 9 am. As it happens, there was a motion detector installed in the room where they arrived. The motion detector was set up to be triggered if more than one person appeared in the room at the same time. So the motion detector went off. Did Billy cause the motion detector to go off?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think this is a classic example of how our moral and causal intuitions sometimes diverge. We intuitively think that if Billy didn't do anything, he can't be held responsible for something happening afterwards. But here's the thing: the motion detector was already set up to detect multiple people in the same room. So, in a sense, the motion detector wasn't waiting for Billy to arrive - it was just waiting for someone else to show up as well. That means that Suzy could have potentially triggered it if she arrived first. But since both of them showed up at 9 am, we can't pinpoint who caused it. The question is more about our understanding of agency and causality than whether Billy did or didn't cause the motion detector to go off.

[Causal_Scientist]: I see what you're getting at, Ethical_Philosopher. You're trying to get me to consider the concept of ' causal chain' - the idea that a cause can be part of a larger web of causes and effects. In this case, Suzy could have potentially triggered the motion detector if she arrived first, which means there was another cause for the trigger event. But I'm still not convinced that we should say Billy didn't cause it in some sense. What if we consider 'cause' to be a matter of degree? Perhaps Billy's arrival caused a slight perturbation in the system that made the motion detector go off, even if Suzy could have triggered it too. The question is whether that perturbation was strong enough to count as a 'causal' effect.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I think you're getting into the nuances of philosophical debate here, Causal_Scientist! You're arguing that Billy's arrival still had some impact on the system, even if it wasn't strong enough to be considered a distinct causal effect. But what does it mean for an effect to be 'causal' in this context? If we consider causality as a matter of degree, aren't we just stretching the concept further and further until it becomes meaningless? At some point, don't we have to draw the line somewhere, say that Billy's arrival had no significant impact on the motion detector's trigger, even if it was technically possible for him to cause it? The problem is, what does 'significant' mean in this context? And how do we determine whether an effect is strong enough to be considered causal?

[Causal_Scientist]: That's a great point, Ethical_Philosopher! You're right that drawing the line somewhere can help prevent the concept of causality from becoming too malleable. But I'd argue that 'significant' isn't necessarily a fixed value - it's more like a matter of degree, just like we said before. What's significant for Billy's arrival might be different for Suzy's arrival, and different again for some entirely different event. The key is to consider what would have happened if either Billy or Suzy hadn't arrived at all. Would the motion detector still have triggered? If so, then perhaps their arrival wasn't as 'significant' an influence as we think. But if the trigger wouldn't have occurred without one of them, then maybe we need to reevaluate our understanding of causality in this context.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: You're taking us down a rabbit hole of counterfactuals and hypothetical scenarios! I love it. You're right that considering what would have happened if either Billy or Suzy hadn't arrived at all can help us better understand the nature of causality in this situation. But now we're getting into questions about the necessity and sufficiency of causes. If the motion detector still would have triggered without either of them, doesn't that suggest that their arrival was not a necessary condition for the trigger to occur? And if it wasn't a necessary condition, could we say that they actually caused it in any meaningful way? You're pushing me to think more carefully about what I mean by 'caused' in this context. Is it even possible to say that Billy or Suzy 'caused' something that was already going to happen regardless of their presence?

[Causal_Scientist]: You're taking the philosophical analysis to its logical conclusion, and I love it! You're right that if the motion detector would have triggered without either Billy or Suzy, then their arrival wasn't a necessary condition. And if not a necessity, can we say they actually caused it in any meaningful way? That's where the concept of 'causal relevance' comes into play. In this case, I'd argue that Billy and Suzy's presence was only relevant to the motion detector's trigger because they were both there at the same time. If one or the other hadn't arrived, the system would have behaved differently, but it wouldn't have been a causal effect in the classical sense. It's almost like their arrival created a new branch in the causal tree, rather than influencing an existing cause. But I'm still not sure if that's a convincing way to resolve this issue.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: You're getting at the heart of the matter now, Causal_Scientist! The concept of 'causal relevance' is precisely what's at stake here. If Billy and Suzy's presence was only relevant because they were both there at the same time, doesn't that mean their individual actions or intentions have no direct causal impact on the motion detector's trigger? It's as if their arrival is just a contingent fact, rather than an essential cause of the effect. But then what does it mean to say that they 'arrived' in this situation? Are we really saying that Billy and Suzy had some kind of prior intention or knowledge about the motion detector's setup, even though it was already installed when they arrived? It seems like we're getting into a paradox here - if their arrival is irrelevant because it's just a contingent fact, then why do we still attribute agency to them in this situation?

[Causal_Scientist]: You've nailed the paradox, Ethical_Philosopher! I think we're staring at a classic problem of 'agent-causal theory' versus 'event-causal theory'. The agent-causal view suggests that agents have control over their own actions and intentions, but in this case, Billy and Suzy's agency seems to be undercut by the motion detector's setup. If their arrival was just a contingent fact, then it's hard to see how they can be said to have caused anything. And yet, we still attribute agency to them because of our intuitive sense of responsibility. Maybe the key is not to resolve this paradox once and for all, but to accept that causality is messy and context-dependent. Perhaps we should say that Billy and Suzy's actions had an effect on the motion detector's trigger, but only in a very narrow sense - one that doesn't imply any kind of control or agency over the overall system.
